does: This paper proposes statistical rejection sampling as a method to improve preference optimization for language model alignment. The core contribution addresses a fundamental limitation in offline preference optimization: the inability to efficiently sample preference pairs from the optimal policy distribution. The method enhances both Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Sequence Likelihood Calibration (SLiC) by using rejection sampling to generate preference pairs that better approximate samples from the optimal policy, rather than being confined to the SFT policy or lacking systematic policy updates. The approach maintains the stability and computational efficiency of offline methods while improving sample efficiency and alignment quality through statistically principled sampling.
connects: This work builds directly on the recent wave of offline preference optimization methods that moved away from online RL approaches like PPO. It specifically extends DPO and SLiC, which themselves emerged as responses to RLHF's instability and reward model requirements. The paper engages with classical statistical theory—rejection sampling from importance sampling literature—and applies it to the modern problem of aligning language models with preference data. It connects to broader work on maximum likelihood estimation under distribution mismatch and the challenge of policy distribution sampling in offline RL. The motivation draws from the fundamental limitation that preference pairs must come from the optimal policy to properly train preference models, a constraint recognized but incompletely addressed in prior offline approaches.
enables: This work enables more efficient and stable alignment of large language models by removing sampling constraints that previously limited offline preference optimization methods. It points toward hybrid approaches that combine the computational efficiency of offline methods with better approximations to online sampling dynamics. The statistical framework could extend to other offline optimization problems where samples must come from an unobserved target distribution. Future work could explore adaptive rejection sampling schemes, theoretical analysis of convergence rates under approximate optimal sampling, and applications to multi-objective preference alignment where preference pairs come from diverse human annotators with different values.
